evidence: Error wrapping is inconsistent across core modules: some paths preserve causes with `%w` while many sibling paths use `%v` and lose unwrap semantics (e.g., `goseg/system/disk.go:180`, `goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go:148`, `goseg/logger/logger.go:280`).

Error classification relies on message text matching in multiple subsystems (`goseg/docker/lifecycle/lifecycle.go:453`, `goseg/importer/importer.go:1034`) instead of typed/sentinel checks, making behavior fragile to wording changes.

Operational failures are handled differently for similar side effects: some functions return joined errors, while others only log and continue (`goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go:163-189`, `goseg/routines/logstream/logs.go:390-393`).
At least one boundary uses brittle string equality for EOF detection (`goseg/routines/logstream/logs.go:333`) rather than `errors.Is(err, io.EOF)`, creating inconsistent failure handling patterns.

findings.summary: Cross-module error wrapping alternates between `%w` and `%v`, causing unpredictable loss of causal chains.
evidence: `goseg/system/disk.go:180` and `:184` return `fmt.Errorf(...: %v, err)` while nearby code uses `%w` (`:269`).
`goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go:148`, `:237`, `:402` use `%v` for wrapped operational errors while sibling paths in the same file use `%w` (`:320`, `:327`, `:334`).
findings.suggestion: Standardize to `%w` whenever returning an existing error, reserve `%v` for logging only, and add a lint/check (or shared helper) to enforce wrapping consistency at return boundaries.

findings.summary: Error handling decisions depend on brittle substring matching instead of typed/sentinel errors.
evidence: `goseg/docker/lifecycle/lifecycle.go:449-453` treats any error containing "not found" as a missing container.
`goseg/importer/importer.go:1027-1035` ignores cleanup failures by scanning `err.Error()` for "not found"/"no such".

Siblings emit different untyped not-found messages (`goseg/docker/lifecycle/lifecycle.go:518` "Container not found" vs `goseg/shipworkflow/operations_runtime.go:328` "status doesn't exist"), making cross-package classification ad hoc.
findings.suggestion: Introduce shared sentinel/typed not-found errors at docker/import/workflow boundaries and replace message parsing with `errors.Is` checks, while mapping low-level provider errors once at boundary adapters.

findings.summary: Several operational write/restart failures are logged but not propagated, creating inconsistent success semantics.
evidence: `goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go:163-189` logs container start/stop failures and still returns nil from the transition step.
`goseg/routines/logstream/logs.go:390-393` file deletion failures are printed and ignored, and the function returns success.
`goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/docker.go:244-246` transition broadcast failures are downgraded to warn-only while other transition failures can trip hard failure thresholds (`:185-190`).
findings.suggestion: Define per-operation error policy (critical vs best-effort) and encode it explicitly via return types or wrapped policy errors; propagate non-best-effort failures consistently and aggregate best-effort failures into structured warnings.

findings.summary: EOF handling in log splitting uses string equality and shadowed error variables, making boundary behavior fragile.
evidence: In the same loop, `err` is reassigned by `chunkWriter.WriteString` (`:353`), so later EOF checks (`:359`) do not refer to the read error anymore.
Elsewhere in scope, error handling already expects wrapped/sentinel-style checks (`goseg/docker/lifecycle/lifecycle.go:449-453`), highlighting inconsistency in boundary error strategy.
findings.suggestion: Use separate `readErr`/`writeErr` variables, switch EOF logic to `errors.Is(readErr, io.EOF)`, and return wrapped context for non-EOF read/write failures.
